file_name: NETGEAR,INC_04_21_2003-EX-10.16- AMENDMENT #2 TO THE DISTRIBUTION AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: For the purposes of this provision, distributor shall mean a company acquiring products directly from NETGEAR for resale or license to dealers or other second tier resellers which in turn resell or license the products to end use customers.
perturbation.changed_text: For the purposes of this provision, distributor shall mean a company acquiring products directly from NETGEAR for resale or license to dealers or other second tier resellers which in turn resell or license the products to any customers.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies 'end use customers.' The modified text broadens this to 'any customers,' potentially including entities that are not 'end users,' which conflicts with standard definitions used in distribution agreements and antitrust law regarding the distribution chain.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Sherman Act prohibits contracts, combinations, and conspiracies in restraint of trade. By changing the definition of 'distributor' to include sales to any customer, the agreement could potentially restrain trade by limiting NETGEAR's ability to sell directly to end-users, creating a possible antitrust violation because it may be viewed as an exclusive dealing arrangement that harms competition. The original agreement's reference to 'end use customers' is a standard limitation that this change removes, expanding the potential for anticompetitive effects.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty U.S. Code Notes prev | next Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be deemed guilty of a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding $100,000,000 if a corporation, or, if any other person, $1,000,000, or by imprisonment not exceeding 10 years, or by both said punishments, in the discretion of the court. (July 2, 1890, ch. 647, § 1, 26 Stat. 209 ; Aug. 17, 1937, ch. 690, title VIII, 50 Stat. 693 ; July 7, 1955, ch. 281, 69 Stat. 282 ; Pub. L. 93–528, § 3 , Dec. 21, 1974 , 88 Stat. 1708 ; Pub. perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the definition of 'distributor' from selling to 'end use customers' to 'any customers'. This change could potentially restrain trade by limiting NETGEAR's ability to sell directly to end-users, creating a possible antitrust violation. The Sherman Act, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, declares illegal 'Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce'. By removing the 'end use' limitation, the agreement increases the potential for anticompetitive effects, thus contradicting the Sherman Act.

perturbation.original_text: The terms and conditions of this Amendment, shall amend and supersede any conflicting terms of the original Agreement. All other terms of the original Agreement shall remain unchanged.
perturbation.changed_text: The terms and conditions of this Amendment, shall amend and supersede any terms of the original Agreement. All other terms of the original Agreement are null and void.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that only conflicting terms are superseded, keeping the rest of the agreement intact. The modified text states that all other terms are null and void. This contradicts general contract law principles and the explicit intention of the parties to only modify specific parts of the original agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-207 addresses the 'battle of the forms' and provides rules for determining which terms become part of a contract when parties exchange differing forms. The modified text directly conflicts with the principle that consistent terms from prior agreements remain in effect unless explicitly superseded. Declaring all other terms 'null and void' undermines established contractual expectations and trade practices. UCC favors upholding as much of the original agreement as possible unless specific contradictions arise.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-207. Additional Terms in Acceptance or Confirmation. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-207. Additional Terms in Acceptance or Confirmation. (1) A definite and seasonable expression of acceptance or a written confirmation which is sent within a reasonable time operates as an acceptance even though it states terms additional to or different from those offered or agreed upon, unless acceptance is expressly made conditional on assent to the additional or different terms. (2) The additional terms are to be construed as proposals for addition to the contract . Between merchants such terms become part of the contract unless: (a) the offer expressly limits acceptance to the terms of the offer; (b) they materially alter it; or (c) notification of objection to them has already been given or is given within a reasonable time after notice of them is received. (3) Conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of a contract is sufficient to establish a contract for sale although the writings of the parties do not otherwise establish a contract. In such case the terms of the particular contract consist of those terms on which the writings of the parties agree, together with any supplementary terms incorporated under any other provisions of this Act. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a contradiction by stating that 'all other terms of the original Agreement are null and void.' This directly opposes the principle outlined in UCC § 2-207, which favors upholding as much of the original agreement as possible unless specific contradictions arise. Snippet 1, § 2-207, states that 'A definite and seasonable expression of acceptance or a written confirmation which is sent within a reasonable time operates as an acceptance even though it states terms additional to or different from those offered or agreed upon.' The changed text doesn't just add different terms; it invalidates all existing terms, which is a more drastic action than the UCC envisions. This is a clear contradiction as it undermines established contractual expectations by negating the entire original agreement instead of selectively amending conflicting terms.

perturbation.original_text: This Amendment shall remain in effect for the current term and any renewal term of the Agreement. Notwithstanding the foregoing, all other provisions of the Agreement remain unchanged.
perturbation.changed_text: This Amendment shall remain in effect at Vendor's discretion. Notwithstanding the foregoing, all other provisions of the Agreement remain unchanged.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies the term of the amendment is tied to the original agreement's term and renewals. The modified version makes the duration depend on the 'Vendor's discretion,' which could conflict with contract law principles related to mutual agreement and certainty of terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 33 of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts states that a contract must have reasonably certain terms. Leaving the duration of the amendment to the vendor's discretion introduces an element of uncertainty that could render the amendment unenforceable. The original language provides a defined term, while the modified language allows the vendor to terminate the agreement at any time, making the duration indeterminate and potentially voiding the agreement due to lack of mutual obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-305. Open Price Term. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-305. Open Price Term. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) nothing is said as to price; or (b) the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith . (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract . In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a term tied to the original agreement's term with 'Vendor's discretion,' which introduces uncertainty regarding the duration of the amendment. According to the LLM's law explanation, Restatement (Second) of Contracts, Section 33, states contracts need reasonably certain terms. While snippet 1 discusses open price terms, it does not directly contradict the modification, but the principle highlighted by the LLM is still relevant here, where the duration, not the price, is left open. The change from a defined term to vendor discretion directly contradicts the requirement for certainty in contractual terms, establishing a legal contradiction under the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition.
